scenario: A team builds a custom Connector. They want to write unit tests for the Connector's execute() method -- verify behaviour given various inputs without needing a live Zeebe cluster.
question: Does the Connector SDK provide test utilities?

options.text: " Builder-style test-context helpers in the SDK's test-jar enable unit-testing Connector execute methods directly with simulated input variables and secrets, without any cluster dependency. This enables fast feedback loops during development.

Yes -- the Connector SDK supports unit testing of Connectors without a live Zeebe cluster. connector:connector-runtime-core test-jar -- typical names are OutboundConnectorContextBuilder and InboundConnectorContextBuilder, not a class literally called MockOutboundConnectorContext). The pattern: build a test context, call your Connector's @Operation-annotated method directly, assert outputs / thrown exceptions. JUnit (Java) / Spring Boot Test apply.

Connector behaviour can only be verified through integration tests against a running Camunda 8 cluster -- this understates the unit testing capabilities. While integration tests provide valuable end-to-end validation of Connector + BPMN integration, unit tests using the SDK's test-context builders are faster, cheaper, and capable of covering edge cases and mocked external service responses that are difficult to replicate in a full cluster environment. A combined strategy of unit tests plus integration tests provides the best coverage.

Test utilities in the Connector SDK are provided only for Inbound Connectors and outbound Connector testing requires a live cluster -- this misrepresents the SDK's testing support by limiting it to one direction. Both Outbound and Inbound Connector types have test-context builder utilities in the SDK's test artifact. Outbound Connectors use OutboundConnectorContextBuilder style helpers to simulate variable inputs and secret resolution; Inbound Connectors have analogous helpers for testing their activate lifecycle and correlation logic.

a.text: Incorrect. The Connector SDK supports unit-testing Connectors without a live Zeebe cluster. Per connector-sdk.md:26 the SDK explicitly markets "convenient testing" alongside the runtime APIs (caveat: the doc anchor #testing is broken in 8.8 and the canonical test-helper builders ship in the SDK's test-jar -- exact class names are versioned in the camunda/connectors GitHub repo, not the 8.8 docs). The canonical pattern uses builder-style test-context helpers, not a class called MockOutboundConnectorContext. Typical Java pattern (illustrative -- adjust class names to whichever the SDK version exposes): `java // Illustrative -- actual builder class lives in connector-runtime-core test artifact @Test void testHttpConnectorExecute() { var context = OutboundConnectorContextBuilder.create() .variables("{\"url\":\"https://example.com/api\",\"method\":\"POST\"}") .secret("API_KEY", "test-secret-value") .build(); var connector = new MyHttpConnector(); var result = connector.execute(context); assertThat(result).isNotNull(); } Benefits: Fast feedback during development. Coverage: test edge cases (invalid input, mocked partner failures, etc.). CI: integrate into normal test pipeline. No cluster required: unit-level isolation. Combine with integration tests (against a real Camunda cluster) for end-to-end verification of Connector + BPMN integration. For Inbound Connectors, similar test utilities exist for testing the activate` lifecycle and event correlation.

c.text: Incorrect. Integration tests valuable; unit tests faster.
